method:
1. Clean and cut tuna into large cubes
2. Soak goraka in warm water for 10 minutes, then grind to paste
3. Coat fish with goraka paste, turmeric, pepper and salt
4. Place in a clay pot with curry leaves and pandan leaves
5. Cook on low heat without water for 30-40 minutes, turning occasionally
6. The fish will release its own moisture and become dark and dry
tips: Use thick tuna steaks for best results. Do not add water - the fish cooks in its own juices.
cultural_note: Ambulthiyal is the signature dish of Southern Sri Lanka, especially Galle and Matara. The goraka acts as a natural preservative, allowing the dish to last several days without refrigeration.

method:
1. Clean crabs, remove top shell, cut in half and crack claws
2. Heat oil, add fenugreek seeds until they pop
3. Saute onions, garlic, ginger until golden
4. Add tomatoes, curry powder, chili, turmeric, cook 5 mins
5. Add crabs and tamarind paste, stir to coat
6. Pour in coconut milk, add curry leaves, cover and simmer 25-30 minutes
tips: Select heavy crabs with firm shells for the meatiest results. Crack claws before cooking so the curry penetrates.
cultural_note: Jaffna crab curry is world-renowned and considered one of Sri Lanka's finest dishes. The Jaffna Peninsula's lagoons provide exceptional mud crabs that make this curry uniquely flavorful.

method:
1. Marinate mutton with curry powder, chili, turmeric, salt for 30 mins
2. Heat oil, add whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon) until fragrant
3. Saute onions, garlic, ginger until brown
4. Add marinated mutton, cook on high heat for 10 minutes
5. Add tomatoes and thin coconut milk, cover and simmer 60 mins
6. Add potatoes and thick coconut milk, cook 20 more minutes until tender
tips: Slow cooking on low heat is essential. The mutton should be falling off the bone when done.
cultural_note: Mutton curry is a celebration dish in Sri Lanka, commonly served at weddings, religious festivals, and special occasions across all communities.

method:
1. Cut jackfruit into small pieces, remove seeds
2. Boil jackfruit with turmeric and salt until half cooked
3. Heat oil, add mustard and fenugreek seeds until they pop
4. Saute onions and garlic until soft
5. Add spices, Maldive fish, curry leaves, pandan leaves
6. Add jackfruit and coconut milk, simmer 25-30 minutes until thick
tips: Oil your hands before cutting raw jackfruit to prevent sticky latex from sticking to your skin.
cultural_note: Polos curry is a beloved vegetarian curry that mimics the texture of meat. It is a staple during the Buddhist Lent period when many Sri Lankans avoid meat.

method:
1. Wash pea eggplants and pierce each one with a knife
2. Heat oil, saute onions, garlic, green chilies
3. Add curry powder, turmeric, Maldive fish
4. Add eggplants, goraka, and thin coconut milk
5. Cook on medium heat 20 minutes until eggplants are soft
6. Add thick coconut milk, simmer 5 minutes
tips: Pierce the eggplants so curry flavor penetrates inside. Use goraka for authentic sour taste.
cultural_note: Ela batu is a tiny variety of eggplant unique to Sri Lanka and South India. This curry is considered very healthy and is a common accompaniment to rice and curry meals.

method:
1. Cut chicken into pieces, marinate with curry powder, turmeric, salt
2. Heat oil, add cinnamon and cardamom
3. Saute onions until deep golden brown
4. Add garlic, ginger, tomato, chili powder
5. Add chicken, cook 10 mins on high, then add coconut milk
6. Cover and simmer 30-35 minutes, finish with roasted curry powder
tips: Use village (free-range) chicken for authentic flavor. The meat is tougher but far more flavorful.
cultural_note: This village-style chicken curry from Uva Province uses traditional wood-fire cooking methods. The slow cooking in a clay pot gives it an earthy, smoky flavor unique to rural Sri Lankan kitchens.

method:
1. Wash lentils, boil with turmeric until soft and mushy
2. Heat oil, add mustard and cumin seeds until they pop
3. Saute onions, garlic, green chilies until soft
4. Add tomato, curry leaves, cook 3 minutes
5. Add boiled lentils and washed spinach
6. Pour in coconut milk, simmer 10 minutes, season with salt
tips: Stir the lentils frequently to prevent sticking. The spinach wilts quickly so add it last.
cultural_note: Dhall curry is eaten daily in almost every Sri Lankan household. Adding spinach is a modern nutritional enhancement that many health-conscious families now practice.

method:
1. Soak cashews in water for 2 hours or boil for 15 minutes
2. Heat oil, saute onions and garlic until soft
3. Add curry powder, chili, turmeric, Maldive fish
4. Add drained cashews, stir to coat with spices
5. Pour in thin coconut milk, cook 15-20 minutes
6. Add thick coconut milk, pandan leaf, simmer 5 minutes
tips: Pre-soaking cashews ensures they cook evenly and become creamy.
cultural_note: Cashew curry is a premium dish often served at weddings and special events. Sri Lanka's North Western Province (Puttalam) is the heart of the country's cashew production.

method:
1. Peel and dice beetroot into small cubes
2. Boil beetroot with turmeric until half cooked
3. Heat oil, add mustard seeds until they pop
4. Saute onions, green chilies, curry leaves
5. Add beetroot, curry powder, Maldive fish, coconut
6. Add coconut milk, simmer until beetroot is tender
tips: Wear gloves when handling beetroot to avoid staining your hands.
cultural_note: Beetroot curry is a colorful addition to the traditional rice and curry plate. Its natural sweetness complements the spicier curries on the table.

method:
1. Cut eggplant into thick rounds, sprinkle with turmeric and salt
2. Deep fry eggplant slices until golden, set aside
3. In a pan, add mustard seeds until they pop
4. Saute onions, garlic, ginger, green chilies until caramelized
5. Add vinegar, sugar, chili powder, cook 3 minutes
6. Gently fold in fried eggplant, coat with the sauce
tips: The sweet-sour-spicy balance is key. Adjust vinegar and sugar to your taste.
cultural_note: Brinjal moju is a beloved Sri Lankan pickle-style curry that reflects Dutch colonial influence. It is a must-have at Sinhala and Tamil New Year celebrations.

method:
1. Clean squid, remove ink sac, cut into rings
2. Heat oil, add fenugreek seeds until fragrant
3. Saute onions, garlic, ginger until golden
4. Add tomato, curry powder, chili, turmeric
5. Add squid rings and goraka, cook 5 minutes on high
6. Pour in coconut milk, simmer 15-20 minutes
tips: Do not overcook squid or it becomes rubbery. 15-20 minutes is ideal.
cultural_note: Squid curry is a coastal delicacy especially popular in fishing villages along the southern coast of Sri Lanka.

method:
1. Cut snake gourd into 1-inch pieces, remove seeds
2. Heat oil, add mustard seeds until they pop
3. Saute onions and green chilies
4. Add snake gourd, curry powder, turmeric, Maldive fish
5. Add thin coconut milk, cook 15-20 minutes until soft
6. Add thick coconut milk, simmer 5 minutes
tips: Choose young tender snake gourds for best results.
cultural_note: Snake gourd is one of the most commonly grown vegetables in Sri Lankan home gardens, making this curry a true household staple.

method:
1. Slice bitter gourd thinly, rub with salt and turmeric
2. Squeeze out bitter juice, rinse and drain
3. Deep fry slices until crispy, set aside
4. Saute onions, mustard seeds, curry leaves
5. Add spices, tamarind, sugar, Maldive fish
6. Add fried bitter gourd and coconut milk, simmer 10 minutes
tips: Salting and squeezing removes most of the bitterness while retaining the health benefits.
cultural_note: Karawila is highly valued in Sri Lankan traditional medicine (Ayurveda) for its blood sugar-regulating properties. Despite its bitter taste, it is enjoyed across the island.

method:
1. Clean and tear mushrooms into bite-sized pieces
2. Heat oil, saute onions and garlic
3. Add tomato, curry powder, chili, turmeric, pepper
4. Add mushrooms, stir-fry 3 minutes
5. Pour in coconut milk, add curry leaves
6. Simmer 15 minutes until sauce thickens
tips: Do not wash mushrooms under running water; wipe with a damp cloth instead.
cultural_note: Mushroom cultivation has become increasingly popular in Sri Lanka's hill country, making this curry a modern addition to the traditional rice and curry spread.

method:
1. Peel and cube potatoes
2. Heat oil, add mustard seeds until they pop
3. Saute onions and garlic until golden
4. Add curry powder, chili, turmeric, Maldive fish
5. Add potatoes and thin coconut milk
6. Cover and cook 20 minutes until potatoes are tender
tips: Cut potatoes into uniform sizes for even cooking.
cultural_note: Potato curry is a universal favorite in Sri Lanka, served at every rice and curry meal. The style varies by region - Southern style is drier while Western Province prefers it with more gravy.

method:
1. Rub fish with turmeric and salt, set aside 10 minutes
2. Heat oil, saute onions until brown
3. Add garlic, ginger, tomatoes
4. Add chili powder, chili flakes, goraka
5. Add fish pieces carefully, add vinegar
6. Cook 15-20 minutes on medium heat without stirring
tips: Use firm fish like seer or tuna that won't break apart during cooking.
cultural_note: Malu mirisata is the fiery red fish curry of Sri Lanka's Southern coast. Fishermen's wives traditionally prepare this with the day's fresh catch.

method:
1. Clean and devein prawns, keep tails on
2. Heat oil, add fenugreek seeds
3. Saute onions and garlic until soft
4. Add tomato, all spices, and tamarind paste
5. Add prawns, cook 3-4 minutes until pink
6. Pour in coconut milk, simmer 10 minutes
tips: Do not overcook prawns - they cook very quickly and become tough if overcooked.
cultural_note: Prawn curry is a luxurious dish typically prepared for special occasions. Sri Lanka's coastal areas, especially Negombo, are famous for their fresh prawns.

method:
1. Peel plantains, cut into thick rounds
2. Rub with turmeric and salt
3. Heat oil, add mustard seeds until they pop
4. Saute onions, curry leaves, pandan leaf
5. Add plantain, spices, Maldive fish, thin coconut milk
6. Cook 20 minutes, add thick coconut milk, simmer 5 more minutes
tips: Use mature ash plantains (not sweet bananas) for this curry.
cultural_note: Ash plantain curry is one of the most affordable and beloved vegetable curries in Sri Lanka. It is available year-round and pairs perfectly with rice.

method:
1. Clean cuttlefish, remove bone and ink, cut into pieces
2. Heat oil, add fenugreek, saute onions, garlic, ginger
3. Add tomato, curry powder, chili and turmeric
4. Add cuttlefish, vinegar, cook 5 minutes
5. Pour in thin coconut milk, simmer 20 minutes
6. Add thick coconut milk, curry leaves, cook 5 more minutes
tips: Score the cuttlefish in a crosshatch pattern so it curls beautifully while cooking.
cultural_note: Cuttlefish curry is popular in Trincomalee and Batticaloa on Sri Lanka's eastern coast, where fresh cuttlefish is abundant.

method:
1. Cut green beans into 1-inch pieces
2. Heat oil, add mustard seeds until they pop
3. Saute onions and curry leaves
4. Add beans, spices, Maldive fish, desiccated coconut
5. Add coconut milk, cover and cook 15-20 minutes
6. Season with salt, stir gently
tips: Use fresh tender beans for the best texture.
cultural_note: Green bean curry is one of the simplest yet most satisfying Sri Lankan curries, commonly prepared for daily home meals.

method:
1. Peel pumpkin, cut into bite-sized cubes
2. Heat oil, add mustard seeds
3. Saute onions and curry leaves
4. Add pumpkin, spices, Maldive fish
5. Add coconut milk, cover and cook 20 minutes
6. Add sugar to balance the flavor
tips: Choose orange-fleshed pumpkin for naturally sweet curry.
cultural_note: Pumpkin curry with its natural sweetness provides a wonderful contrast to spicy curries on the Sri Lankan plate.

method:
1. Soak dried fish in water 30 minutes, drain and clean
2. Cut into pieces, fry lightly in oil
3. Saute onions, garlic until brown
4. Add tomato, chili, curry powder, turmeric
5. Add dried fish, goraka, tamarind, thin coconut milk
6. Simmer 20 minutes until sauce thickens
tips: Soak and wash dried fish well to remove excess salt.
cultural_note: Dried fish curry is a pantry staple in Sri Lanka, providing protein in areas far from the coast. It continues to be one of the most economical sources of protein for rural families.

method:
1. Cut drumsticks into 3-inch pieces
2. Heat oil, add mustard seeds
3. Saute onions, garlic, curry leaves
4. Add spices and Maldive fish
5. Add drumsticks and thin coconut milk
6. Cook 25 minutes until drumstick flesh is soft
tips: You eat drumsticks by scraping the soft flesh from the fibrous outer shell with your teeth.
cultural_note: Moringa (murunga) is called a superfood and is grown in almost every Sri Lankan garden. The curry made from its pods is nutritious and medicinal.

method:
1. Clean sardines, rub with turmeric and salt
2. Place in a clay pot with sliced onions, garlic, ginger
3. Add tomato, curry powder, chili, goraka
4. Pour thin coconut milk over fish
5. Cook on medium heat 15-20 minutes
6. Add thick coconut milk, simmer 5 minutes
tips: Do not stir the curry - gently shake the pot to prevent fish from breaking.
cultural_note: Sardine curry is the everyday fish curry of Sri Lanka, affordable and nutritious. It is best cooked in a traditional clay pot.

method:
1. Wash leeks thoroughly, cut into 1-inch pieces
2. Heat oil, add mustard seeds
3. Saute onions and curry leaves
4. Add leeks, spices, Maldive fish, pepper
5. Add coconut milk, cook 15 minutes
6. Season with salt
tips: Clean leeks by slicing lengthwise and washing between layers to remove sand.
cultural_note: Leeks grow prolifically in Sri Lanka's hill country around Nuwara Eliya, making this a beloved vegetable curry of the Central Province.

method:
1. Peel green mangoes, cut into chunks
2. Heat oil, add mustard seeds
3. Saute onions and curry leaves
4. Add mango pieces, spices, Maldive fish
5. Add coconut milk and sugar
6. Simmer 20 minutes until mango is soft
tips: Use unripe, sour mangoes for authentic flavor. The sugar balances the tartness.
cultural_note: Mango curry is a seasonal delicacy prepared during the mango season (May-July). Its sweet-sour flavor is a unique addition to the Sri Lankan spread.

method:
1. Drain canned fish, break into large chunks
2. Heat oil, saute onions, garlic, green chilies
3. Add tomato, curry powder, chili, turmeric
4. Gently add fish chunks
5. Pour in coconut milk, add curry leaves
6. Simmer 10-15 minutes on low heat
tips: Handle the fish gently to keep chunks intact. Do not stir vigorously.
cultural_note: Tinned fish curry is one of Sri Lanka's most popular quick meals, especially among students and working families. It is affectionately known as a comfort food.

method:
1. Hard boil eggs, peel and make small cuts on surface
2. Deep fry eggs until golden, set aside
3. Saute onions, garlic, ginger until soft
4. Add tomato, spices, cook 3 minutes
5. Add thin coconut milk, bring to simmer
6. Add fried eggs, cook in curry 10-15 minutes
tips: Score the eggs so the curry sauce penetrates for better flavor.
cultural_note: Egg curry is an economical protein-rich curry enjoyed across all social classes in Sri Lanka. It is a common alternative when meat is unavailable.

method:
1. Cut chicken into bite-sized pieces, season with salt and turmeric
2. Deep fry chicken pieces until golden, set aside
3. Saute onions, garlic, ginger in hot oil
4. Add capsicum, green chilies, tomato
5. Add soy sauce, tomato sauce, chili powder, chili flakes
6. Toss in fried chicken, mix well on high heat for 3-4 minutes
tips: High heat stir-frying is essential for the smoky wok flavor Sri Lankans call 'thel piyawuna'.
cultural_note: Devilled dishes are a unique Sri Lankan invention - a fiery fusion of local spices with Chinese stir-fry techniques, popularized by Sri Lanka's Chinese restaurants.

method:
1. Clean and devein prawns
2. Stir fry prawns in hot oil until just pink, remove
3. In same wok, saute onions, garlic, ginger on high heat
4. Add capsicum, green chilies, tomato
5. Add all sauces, chili powder, chili flakes, vinegar
6. Return prawns, toss vigorously for 2-3 minutes
tips: Cook prawns briefly - they continue cooking in residual heat.
cultural_note: Devilled prawns is a star dish at Sri Lankan Chinese restaurants and is a must-order item at celebrations and parties.

method:
1. Boil potatoes until just cooked, peel and cube
2. Heat oil, add mustard seeds until they pop
3. Add onions, green chilies, curry leaves
4. Add chili powder, turmeric, Maldive fish
5. Add potato cubes, toss gently
6. Sprinkle desiccated coconut, mix and serve
tips: Use waxy potatoes that hold their shape after boiling.
cultural_note: Tempered potato is a dry curry preparation that is one of the most common side dishes served with rice and curry at any Sri Lankan meal.

method:
1. Wash dried shrimp and soak for 10 minutes
2. Heat oil, saute onions, garlic, curry leaves
3. Add chili powder, curry powder, turmeric
4. Add shrimp, goraka, and tamarind
5. Pour in coconut milk
6. Simmer 15 minutes until sauce thickens
tips: Soak the dried shrimp to remove excess salt before cooking.
cultural_note: Kooni isso curry is a humble coastal curry that packs incredible umami flavor. It is a staple in fishing communities along Sri Lanka's coast.

method:
1. Pour thin coconut milk into a pot
2. Add sliced onion, garlic, green chilies, curry leaves, pandan
3. Add turmeric, fenugreek seeds, salt
4. Bring to a gentle simmer, cook 10 minutes
5. Add thick coconut milk, simmer 5 more minutes
6. Remove from heat, add lime juice
tips: Never boil vigorously after adding thick coconut milk - it will split.
cultural_note: Kiri hodi is the gentle coconut milk gravy that accompanies hoppers, string hoppers, and pittu. It is a mild, soothing curry that balances the spicier dishes.

method:
1. Wash lentils until water runs clear
2. Boil with turmeric and salt until soft
3. Heat oil, temper mustard and cumin seeds
4. Saute onions, garlic, green chilies
5. Add curry leaves, Maldive fish
6. Combine with lentils, add coconut milk, simmer 5 minutes
tips: Wash lentils thoroughly to remove starch for better texture.
cultural_note: Parippu is arguably Sri Lanka's most essential curry. No rice and curry meal is complete without it. Every Sri Lankan learns to cook this first.

method:
1. Marinate lamb with spices for 1 hour
2. Heat oil, add whole spices until fragrant
3. Brown onions, garlic, ginger
4. Add marinated lamb, sear on all sides
5. Add tomatoes, thin coconut milk, simmer 1 hour
6. Add thick coconut milk, cook 20 more minutes until tender
tips: Marinating overnight gives the deepest flavor penetration.
cultural_note: Lamb curry is a celebratory dish in Sri Lanka's Muslim communities, especially in the Eastern Province, prepared for Eid and other festivals.

method:
1. Wash gotukola leaves thoroughly
2. Finely chop the leaves
3. Finely chop onion and green chilies
4. Mix all ingredients together
5. Add desiccated coconut, Maldive fish, turmeric
6. Season with lime juice and salt
tips: This is a raw preparation - no cooking needed. The lime juice 'cooks' the leaves slightly.
cultural_note: Gotukola (Centella asiatica) is revered in Ayurvedic medicine for improving brain function. This salad-like curry is eaten daily by health-conscious Sri Lankans.

method:
1. Clean duck, cut into pieces, marinate with vinegar and spices
2. Heat oil, add cinnamon, cardamom, mustard seeds
3. Brown onions, garlic, ginger
4. Add duck pieces, sear until browned
5. Add coconut milk, curry leaves, cover and cook 60 minutes
6. Uncover, reduce sauce until thick
tips: Duck releases a lot of fat during cooking - skim excess fat for a lighter curry.
cultural_note: Duck curry is a special occasion dish in Sri Lanka, particularly popular in the North Western Province where duck farming is common.

method:
1. Soak soya chunks in hot water 15 minutes, squeeze dry
2. Deep fry soya chunks until golden, set aside
3. Saute onions, garlic, cinnamon
4. Add tomato, curry powder, chili, turmeric
5. Add fried soya chunks and coconut milk
6. Simmer 15 minutes until sauce is absorbed
tips: Squeeze out all water from soaked soya for better texture and flavor absorption.
cultural_note: Soya meat curry is a popular vegetarian protein alternative in Sri Lanka, especially during Buddhist Poya days when many avoid meat.

method:
1. Drain canned fish, flake into chunks
2. Heat oil, saute onions until crispy
3. Add green chilies, curry leaves
4. Add chili powder, flakes, turmeric, Maldive fish
5. Add fish, gently toss with spices
6. Add coconut, lime juice, mix and serve
tips: This is meant to be a dry curry - do not add coconut milk.
cultural_note: This quick dry curry is a favorite among Sri Lankan students and bachelors for its simplicity and bold flavors.

method:
1. Peel breadfruit, remove core, cut into chunks
2. Boil breadfruit with turmeric until half cooked
3. Heat oil, add mustard and fenugreek seeds
4. Saute onions, curry leaves
5. Add breadfruit, spices, Maldive fish, coconut milk
6. Simmer 20 minutes until tender and sauce thickens
tips: Choose firm breadfruit that is not overripe for curry.
cultural_note: Breadfruit trees are found in every Sri Lankan village. The curry made from this starchy fruit is filling and economical, often replacing rice during times of scarcity.

method:
1. Marinate venison with vinegar, spices, garlic, ginger overnight
2. Heat oil, add whole spices
3. Brown onions deeply
4. Add marinated meat, sear on high heat
5. Add thin coconut milk, simmer 90 minutes
6. Add thick coconut milk, pepper, cook 15 more minutes
tips: Overnight marination is essential to tenderize the gamey meat.
cultural_note: Venison curry is a traditional dish from Sri Lanka's Uva Province where deer farming exists. It has a rich, deep flavor distinct from other meat curries.

method:
1. Boil jackfruit seeds until tender, peel off outer skin
2. Cut seeds in half
3. Heat oil, add mustard seeds
4. Saute onions and curry leaves
5. Add seeds, spices, Maldive fish, coconut milk
6. Simmer 20 minutes
tips: Boil seeds until you can easily peel the outer brown skin.
cultural_note: In Sri Lanka, nothing from the jackfruit is wasted - the fruit, seeds, and even the young leaves are all used. This zero-waste approach reflects traditional sustainability.

method:
1. Clean liver, cut into bite-sized pieces
2. Heat oil, saute onions, garlic, ginger
3. Add tomato, curry powder, chili, turmeric
4. Add liver, stir-fry 5 minutes on high heat
5. Add coconut milk, vinegar, curry leaves
6. Simmer 15 minutes - do not overcook
tips: A splash of vinegar removes the metallic taste from liver.
cultural_note: Liver curry is a nutritious and affordable dish prized for its high iron content. It is commonly available as a side dish in Sri Lankan rice and curry restaurants.

method:
1. Cut meat into cubes, marinate with vinegar, garlic, ginger, spices overnight
2. Heat oil, add whole spices
3. Brown onions until dark
4. Add marinated meat, sear 10 minutes
5. Add coconut milk, goraka, curry leaves, cover and cook 2 hours
6. Reduce sauce until thick and rich
tips: Long, slow cooking is essential for this tough meat.
cultural_note: Wild boar curry is an ancient Lankan recipe still prepared in rural areas of Sabaragamuwa and Uva. Its intense, gamey flavor is highly prized by meat lovers.

method:
1. Soak chickpeas overnight, boil until tender
2. Heat oil, add mustard and cumin seeds
3. Saute onions, garlic, ginger until golden
4. Add tomato, all spices, cook 5 minutes
5. Add boiled chickpeas, thin coconut milk
6. Simmer 20 minutes, add thick coconut milk last
tips: Soaking overnight with a pinch of baking soda makes chickpeas cook faster.
cultural_note: Kadala curry is a protein-rich vegetarian curry beloved in Northern Province. It is especially popular as an accompaniment to pittu and hoppers.

method:
1. Wash rice, drain
2. Heat oil, fry cashews and raisins, set aside
3. Add whole spices, saute 1 min
4. Add rice, turmeric, salt, stir
5. Add coconut milk and pandan, bring to boil
6. Cover, cook on low 20 mins, fluff with fork, garnish with cashews and raisins
tips: Use good quality basmati for fluffy separate grains.
cultural_note: Yellow rice is the centerpiece of Sri Lankan celebrations - weddings, New Year, and religious ceremonies.

method:
1. Wash and soak rice 30 mins
2. Heat ghee, fry cashews and raisins, remove
3. Saute onions with whole spices until golden
4. Add drained rice, stir 2 mins
5. Add stock, salt, bring to boil
6. Cover tight, cook low 20 mins, garnish
tips: Use real ghee, not butter, for authentic aroma.
cultural_note: Ghee rice is a festive Muslim dish served at weddings and Eid celebrations in Eastern Sri Lanka.

method:
1. Marinate chicken in yogurt, masala, garlic, ginger for 2 hours
2. Parboil rice with whole spices until 70% cooked
3. Deep fry onions until crispy
4. In pot layer: chicken, rice, fried onions, mint, coriander
5. Add saffron milk, ghee, seal with foil
6. Cook on dum (very low heat) 30 mins
tips: Don't open the lid during dum cooking - the steam does the magic.
cultural_note: Sri Lankan biriyani, especially from Akkaraipattu and Kalmunai, has its own unique style distinct from Indian versions.

method:
1. Cook rice in mixed meat stock with spices
2. Prepare all components: beef curry, chicken curry, frikkadels, moju, blachan
3. Soften banana leaves over flame
4. Place rice on banana leaf, add portions of each component
5. Add half a boiled egg
6. Wrap tightly, bake in oven at 180C for 20 mins
tips: Each component must be prepared separately for authentic lamprais.
cultural_note: Lamprais is Sri Lanka's most famous Dutch Burgher dish, derived from the Dutch 'lomprijst'. It is a complete meal wrapped in banana leaf.

method:
1. Wash rice thoroughly
2. Cook rice with water until half done
3. Add thick coconut milk and salt
4. Stir gently and cook until milk is absorbed
5. Press into flat tray, smooth the surface
6. Cut into diamond shapes when slightly cool
tips: Use full-fat thick coconut milk for creamy results.
cultural_note: Kiribath is the first food cooked in a new year and for every auspicious occasion. It symbolizes prosperity and new beginnings in Sri Lankan culture.

method:
1. Toast desiccated coconut in dry pan until golden
2. Wash rice, add to pot with coconut milk and water
3. Add pandan, curry leaves, salt
4. Cook until done
5. Fold in toasted coconut
6. Garnish with fried cashews
tips: Toast coconut on low heat, stirring constantly to avoid burning.
cultural_note: Coconut rice is a fragrant Southern Sri Lankan specialty that highlights the island's abundance of coconut.

method:
1. Use day-old cold rice for best results
2. Heat oil on high, scramble eggs, remove
3. Stir-fry garlic, onions, green chilies
4. Add all vegetables, cook 3 mins on high
5. Add rice, soy sauce, toss vigorously
6. Add eggs back, mix well
tips: Day-old refrigerated rice makes the best fried rice - less sticky.
cultural_note: Chinese-style fried rice has been adopted as a Sri Lankan staple, served at every restaurant and party.

method:
1. Dice chicken, season with salt and pepper
2. Stir-fry chicken on high heat, set aside
3. Scramble eggs, set aside
4. Stir-fry all vegetables with garlic and ginger
5. Add cold rice, soy sauce, oyster sauce
6. Return chicken and egg, toss on high heat
tips: Cut chicken into small uniform pieces for even cooking.
cultural_note: Chicken fried rice is the most popular single dish ordered at Sri Lankan Chinese restaurants.

method:
1. Beat eggs lightly with salt
2. Heat oil on high, pour in eggs, scramble quickly
3. Add garlic and onions, stir-fry 1 min
4. Add cold rice, break up lumps
5. Add soy sauce, white pepper
6. Garnish with green onions
tips: High heat and quick movements are key to good fried rice.
cultural_note: Egg fried rice is Sri Lanka's most affordable fried rice option, popular with students.

method:
1. Clean prawns and squid, slice squid into rings
2. Stir-fry seafood on high heat 2 mins, remove
3. Scramble eggs, remove
4. Stir-fry vegetables with garlic
5. Add rice, soy sauce, fish sauce
6. Return seafood and eggs, toss well
tips: Cook seafood briefly on very high heat.
cultural_note: Seafood fried rice is a premium dish at Sri Lankan restaurants, often served at parties.

method:
1. Dice chicken, stir-fry with prawns, set aside
2. Scramble eggs, set aside
3. Saute onions, garlic, chili paste
4. Add rice, soy sauces, toss on high heat
5. Return chicken, prawns, eggs
6. Serve topped with fried egg, cucumber, shallots
tips: Use kecap manis (sweet soy) for authentic flavor.
cultural_note: Nasi Goreng reflects the Malay cultural influence on Sri Lankan cuisine, particularly in Colombo.

method:
1. Blend 2 tomatoes, dice remaining 2
2. Heat oil, saute onions, garlic, cinnamon
3. Add diced tomatoes, cook 3 mins
4. Add rice, blended tomato, water, spices
5. Bring to boil, cover, cook on low 20 mins
6. Fluff with fork
tips: Use ripe red tomatoes for best color and flavor.
cultural_note: Tomato rice is a quick one-pot meal popular for lunch boxes and picnics.

method:
1. Break string hoppers into small pieces
2. Heat oil, fry cashews, set aside
3. Saute onions with whole spices
4. Add curry powder, turmeric, peas
5. Add string hopper pieces, toss gently
6. Scramble eggs into mixture, garnish with cashews
tips: Use slightly dry string hoppers for better texture.
cultural_note: String hopper pilau is a creative Jaffna innovation that repurposes leftover string hoppers into a flavorful one-dish meal.

method:
1. Marinate mutton in yogurt and spices
2. Cook marinated mutton until tender
3. Parboil rice with whole spices
4. Deep fry onions until crispy
5. Layer meat, rice, onions, mint in pot, drizzle saffron milk and rose water
6. Seal and cook on very low heat 25 mins
tips: The rose water and saffron are what distinguish Malay buriyani.
cultural_note: Sri Lankan Malay buriyani is a heritage recipe brought by Malay soldiers during Dutch colonial times.

method:
1. Cook rice and cool
2. Heat sesame oil, add mustard seeds, dried chilies
3. Add peanuts, curry leaves, saute
4. Add tamarind paste, turmeric, salt, cook 2 mins
5. Add cooled rice, mix thoroughly
6. Serve at room temperature
tips: Spreading cooked rice on a tray to cool prevents it from becoming mushy.
cultural_note: Tamarind rice is a Tamil dish popular in Northern Sri Lanka, often prepared for temple offerings.

method:
1. Marinate prawns in yogurt, masala, lime juice
2. Parboil rice with whole spices
3. Deep fry onions, set aside
4. Cook marinated prawns 5 mins
5. Layer rice, prawns, onions, mint in pot
6. Seal, cook on dum 20 mins
tips: Do not overcook prawns before layering.
cultural_note: Prawn biriyani from Batticaloa is considered one of the finest rice dishes in Sri Lanka.

method:
1. Wash rice thoroughly
2. Add water and pandan leaves, cook until water is absorbed
3. Pour in thick coconut milk
4. Stir gently on low heat
5. Cook until all milk is absorbed and rice is creamy
6. Press flat, cut into pieces
tips: Samba rice gives a stickier, more traditional texture than basmati.
cultural_note: This variation uses samba rice for a more rustic, village-style milk rice.

method:
1. Slice mushrooms, saute in butter until brown
2. Saute onions and garlic
3. Add rice, stir 2 minutes
4. Add stock, soy sauce, pepper, thyme
5. Bring to boil, cover, cook low 20 mins
6. Fluff and fold in mushrooms
tips: Cook mushrooms separately for best caramelization.
cultural_note: Mushroom rice is a modern Sri Lankan dish popular in hill country restaurants.

method:
1. Marinate fish in yogurt, masala, lime juice 30 mins
2. Parboil rice, drain
3. Fry onions crispy, set aside half
4. Cook marinated fish with tomatoes 10 mins
5. Layer rice, fish curry, onions, herbs
6. Seal with foil, dum cook 20 mins
tips: Use firm fish fillets that won't break during cooking.
cultural_note: Fish biriyani is a specialty of coastal Eastern Province towns.

method:
1. Prepare lunu miris (chili, onion, lime, Maldive fish, salt)
2. Heat oil, saute onions and curry leaves
3. Add Maldive fish
4. Add cooked rice, toss on high heat
5. Mix in lunu miris thoroughly
6. Serve hot
tips: Use freshly ground lunu miris for best flavor.
cultural_note: A quick, fiery rice dish inspired by the flavors of Southern Sri Lanka.

method:
1. Wash rice, soak 15 mins
2. Heat ghee, fry cashews and raisins
3. Add whole spices, saute 1 min
4. Add rice, stir gently
5. Add water, salt, bring to boil
6. Cover, cook low 20 mins
tips: Use Ceylon cinnamon for the most authentic sweet aroma.
cultural_note: Sri Lanka is the world's largest producer of true cinnamon. This rice celebrates that heritage.

names.english: Red Rice and Curry (Rathu Kekulu Bath)

method:
1. Wash red rice well
2. Add water and salt
3. Bring to a boil
4. Reduce to low heat, cover tightly
5. Cook 30-35 minutes until tender
6. Fluff with fork and serve with curries
tips: Red rice takes longer to cook than white rice - be patient.
cultural_note: Red rice is the traditional staple grain of Sri Lanka, considered more nutritious than polished white rice. It has a nutty flavor and chewy texture.

method:
1. Tear or chop roti into small pieces
2. Stir-fry chicken until cooked, set aside
3. Scramble eggs, set aside
4. Stir-fry vegetables on high heat
5. Add roti pieces, curry sauce, soy sauce, chili
6. Toss vigorously with spatulas on hot griddle, add chicken and egg
tips: The rhythmic chopping sound on the hot griddle is signature of authentic kottu.
cultural_note: Kottu roti is Sri Lanka's most iconic street food. The rhythmic clanging of metal spatulas on the griddle can be heard from hotels across the island every evening.

method:
1. Mix rice flour with salt
2. Sprinkle water gradually, mix until flour forms crumbles
3. Layer flour and coconut alternately in pittu mold
4. Steam for 10-15 minutes
5. Push out of mold
6. Serve with coconut milk curry or fish curry
tips: The flour should feel like damp sand - not wet, not dry.
cultural_note: Pittu is unique to Sri Lanka and parts of South India. This cylindrical steamed rice dish is traditionally made using a bamboo mold.

method:
1. Dice all vegetables small
2. Heat oil, add mustard seeds and curry leaves
3. Saute vegetables with spices and Maldive fish
4. Add coconut milk, cook until vegetables are tender
5. Mix in cooked rice, toss gently
6. Season and serve
tips: Cut vegetables into very small dice so they blend with the rice.
cultural_note: This mixed rice is a one-pot meal common in Central Province households.

method:
1. Dissolve yeast and sugar in warm water, let froth
2. Mix rice flour, salt, coconut milk, yeast mixture
3. Add coconut cream, mix to thin batter
4. Ferment 6-8 hours or overnight
5. Heat hopper pan, add ladle of batter, swirl to coat
6. Cover and cook 2-3 mins until edges are crispy and center is soft
tips: Overnight fermentation gives the best flavor and texture.
cultural_note: Hoppers are Sri Lanka's most distinctive breakfast dish. The bowl-shaped rice pancake with crispy edges and soft center is found nowhere else in the world.

method:
1. Prepare hopper batter as for plain hoppers
2. Heat hopper pan, add batter, swirl
3. Crack an egg into center of hopper
4. Sprinkle salt and pepper
5. Cover and cook until egg white is set but yolk is runny
6. Serve immediately with sambol
tips: Keep the yolk runny for authentic egg hoppers.
cultural_note: Egg hoppers are the most popular Sri Lankan breakfast. The runny yolk mixed with spicy sambol is heavenly.

method:
1. Boil water with salt
2. Add flour gradually, mix into dough
3. Knead until smooth
4. Fill string hopper press
5. Press in circular motions onto bamboo mats
6. Steam for 5-7 minutes
tips: Knead the dough well - it should be soft and pliable, not crumbly.
cultural_note: String hoppers are a breakfast staple across Sri Lanka, traditionally served with kiri hodi and sambol.

method:
1. Combine flour and salt
2. Add water slowly, rubbing between palms until texture is like wet sand
3. Alternate layers of flour and coconut in pittu mold
4. Attach mold to kettle of boiling water
5. Steam 10-15 mins until steam comes through top
6. Push out of mold, serve with curry
tips: The flour mixture should crumble apart, never form a dough.
cultural_note: Pittu is a staple breakfast made in a cylindrical bamboo mold. Every household has their own pittu bamboo.

method:
1. Mix flour, salt, sugar
2. Add egg and water, knead into soft stretchy dough
3. Oil the dough, rest 30 mins
4. Pull small portions into thin sheets
5. Fold into layers, place on hot griddle with oil
6. Cook both sides until golden and flaky
tips: The dough must be very thin and translucent when stretched.
cultural_note: Godamba roti is Sri Lanka's answer to the Indian paratha, brought by Malay Muslims.

method:
1. Mix both flours, coconut, salt
2. Add chopped onion and green chili
3. Add water slowly to form stiff dough
4. Divide into balls, flatten by hand into thick disks
5. Cook on hot griddle without oil on both sides
6. Press gently to check firmness, serve hot
tips: Do not make the dough too wet - it should be firm and hold its shape.
cultural_note: Pol roti is the simplest, most common breakfast in rural Sri Lanka. It pairs perfectly with seeni sambol and a hot cup of Ceylon tea.

method:
1. Prepare hopper batter
2. Make plain hoppers
3. While still on pan, add sweetened coconut milk to center
4. Cover briefly so milk sets slightly
5. Remove carefully
6. Serve as dessert hopper
tips: The coconut milk should be thick and sweet.
cultural_note: Milk hoppers are the sweet version of Sri Lanka's iconic hopper, often served as dessert.

method:
1. Soak urad dhal 4 hours
2. Grind into thick paste (do not add water)
3. Mix in chopped onion, chili, curry leaves
4. Shape into donuts with hole in center
5. Deep fry in hot oil until golden brown
6. Drain on paper and serve hot
tips: The batter should be thick enough to hold shape. Test by dropping a bit in water - it should float.
cultural_note: Ulundu wade is a beloved Tamil breakfast snack, originating from Jaffna and now popular across the whole island.

method:
1. Prepare dosa batter (fermented rice and urad dhal)
2. Boil and mash potatoes
3. Temper mustard seeds, saute onions, chilies, curry leaves
4. Mix with mashed potato and turmeric
5. Spread thin layer of batter on hot griddle, cook until crispy
6. Place potato filling, fold in half
tips: The batter must be fermented overnight for authentic taste.
cultural_note: Masala dosa is a popular Tamil breakfast in Northern Sri Lanka, with each household having its own family recipe.

method:
1. Soak rice and dhal separately for 6 hours
2. Grind into smooth batter, mix together
3. Add fenugreek and salt, ferment overnight
4. Heat flat griddle, pour ladle of batter
5. Spread in circular motion to thin crepe
6. Cook until bottom is golden, serve with chutney
tips: Fermentation in a warm place for 8-10 hours is essential.
cultural_note: Thosai is a staple Tamil breakfast that is crispy on the outside and soft inside.

method:
1. Cook rice in excess water until very soft
2. Wash and finely chop green leaves
3. Blend leaves with a little water
4. Add leaf puree to cooked rice porridge
5. Add coconut milk, salt
6. Simmer 10 minutes, serve with jaggery
tips: Use a variety of greens for maximum health benefits.
cultural_note: Kola kanda is an ancient Ayurvedic breakfast that has seen a modern revival in Sri Lanka. It is believed to boost immunity and brain power.

method:
1. Mix flour, coconut, chopped onion, chili, salt
2. Add water to form stiff dough
3. Divide into balls
4. Flatten into rounds on a floured surface
5. Cook on hot dry griddle on both sides
6. Serve hot with curry
tips: Use whole wheat atta flour for nutritional value.
cultural_note: Atta roti is the healthier alternative to white flour roti, popular with health-conscious Sri Lankans.

method:
1. Mix millet flour, coconut, chopped onion, salt
2. Add water slowly to make firm dough
3. Shape into flat rounds by hand
4. Cook on hot griddle without oil
5. Cook both sides until firm and slightly charred
6. Serve with fish curry or sambol
tips: Kurakkan dough is crumbly - use wet hands to shape.
cultural_note: Kurakkan (finger millet) roti is a traditional Uva Province breakfast, highly nutritious and gluten-free.

method:
1. Prepare fermented idli batter (rice and urad dhal)
2. Grease idli molds with oil
3. Pour batter into each mold
4. Steam for 10-12 minutes
5. Check with toothpick - it should come out clean
6. Remove with wet spoon, serve with sambar and chutney
tips: Proper fermentation makes idlis fluffy. Over-fermented batter makes sour idlis.
cultural_note: Idli is a quintessential Tamil breakfast, served with sambar and coconut chutney in homes across Jaffna.

method:
1. Dry roast semolina until fragrant, set aside
2. Heat ghee, add mustard seeds, cashews
3. Saute onions, ginger, green chili, curry leaves
4. Add water and salt, bring to boil
5. Slowly add roasted semolina while stirring
6. Cover, cook on low 5 mins, fluff with fork
tips: Stir continuously while adding semolina to prevent lumps.
cultural_note: Upma is a popular South Indian-Tamil breakfast, commonly prepared in Northern Sri Lankan households.

method:
1. Mix rice flour and salt
2. Sprinkle water, rub between palms to form crumbles
3. Fill puttu mold alternating flour and coconut layers
4. Steam 10-12 minutes
5. Push out of mold using wooden press
6. Serve with banana and sugar or fish curry
tips: The flour should be loose and crumbly, never packed tight.
cultural_note: Puttu is the Jaffna variation of pittu, typically served with sweetened coconut or spicy curries.

method:
1. Cook rice with coconut milk and salt into milk rice
2. Press flat on a tray, cut into diamonds
3. Grind chilies, onion, Maldive fish, salt in mortar
4. Add lime juice to the chili paste
5. Serve milk rice diamonds with lunu miris on the side
6. Eat by scooping lunu miris onto each piece
tips: The lunu miris should be freshly ground, not blended.
cultural_note: This is the iconic Sri Lankan breakfast for the first day of every month, and every New Year morning.

names.english: Pani Pol Roti (Sweet Coconut Stuffed Roti)

method:
1. Mix flour, salt, water into soft dough, rest 15 mins
2. Mix grated coconut, grated jaggery, cardamom for filling
3. Roll dough into thin circles
4. Place coconut filling in center, fold edges
5. Flatten gently and cook on hot griddle with oil
6. Cook both sides until golden
tips: Grate the jaggery finely so it melts into the coconut.
cultural_note: Pani pol roti is a sweet breakfast treat that children especially love, filled with treacle-sweetened coconut.

method:
1. Dissolve yeast and sugar in warm water
2. Mix rice flour, coconut milk and yeast mixture
3. Ferment 6-8 hours
4. Heat hopper pan, pour batter and swirl
5. Sprinkle sugar on center while cooking
6. Cover and cook until edges are crispy
tips: Ferment in a warm place for fluffiest results.
cultural_note: Appam is the Tamil version of hoppers, slightly sweeter and often paired with coconut milk.

method:
1. Wash rice, cook until almost done
2. Mix in grated coconut and salt
3. Place mixture on kenda leaves
4. Wrap tightly into cylindrical parcels
5. Steam wrapped parcels 15 minutes
6. Unwrap and serve with curries
tips: Use young tender leaves for wrapping.
cultural_note: Lavariya is a Southern Province specialty where rice is steamed in aromatic wild ginger leaves.

method:
1. Dry roast sesame seeds until golden
2. Grind roasted sesame coarsely
3. Melt jaggery with a splash of water
4. Mix everything together
5. Shape into small balls while warm
6. Serve alongside appam or hoppers for breakfast
tips: Roll quickly while mixture is warm - it hardens as it cools.
cultural_note: Thala guli is a nutritious Tamil sweet snack often served as a breakfast accompaniment.

method:
1. Mix both flours
2. Add coconut milk, sugar, salt to make thick batter
3. Rest batter 15 minutes
4. Heat oil to medium temperature
5. Pour small ladlefuls into oil
6. Fry on low heat until golden and cooked through
tips: Low temperature frying ensures kavum cook through without burning.
cultural_note: Mung kavum are traditional fried cakes originally made for the Sinhala and Tamil New Year.

method:
1. Mash bananas thoroughly
2. Mix in rice flour, sugar, salt, turmeric
3. Form into small flat patties
4. Heat oil to medium
5. Fry patties until golden on both sides
6. Drain and serve warm
tips: Use overripe bananas for natural sweetness.
cultural_note: Banana fritters are the simplest Sri Lankan snack, an easy way to use up ripe bananas that might otherwise be wasted.

method:
1. Wash rice and add to pot with water
2. Add bruised ginger and garlic
3. Bring to boil, then simmer 25-30 minutes
4. Stir occasionally until thick and porridge-like
5. Add coconut milk, salt
6. Serve with dried fish or sambol
tips: The longer you cook, the creamier it gets.
cultural_note: Rice congee is a comfort food breakfast especially during rainy days or when someone is feeling unwell.

method:
1. Remove bread crusts, flatten slices with rolling pin
2. Place spoonful of curry filling on each slice
3. Roll tightly, seal edges with egg wash
4. Dip in beaten egg, then coat with breadcrumbs
5. Deep fry until golden brown
6. Drain and serve hot with tomato sauce
tips: Squeeze the bread rolls firmly to seal so they don't open while frying.
cultural_note: Bread rolls are a classic Sri Lankan short eat, found in every bakery and commonly eaten for breakfast or as a snack.

method:
1. Grate fresh coconut
2. Finely chop red onion
3. Mix coconut, onion, chili flakes, Maldive fish
4. Add lime juice and salt
5. Mix well, crushing slightly with fingers
6. Serve immediately
tips: Use freshly grated coconut, never desiccated, for authentic sambol.
cultural_note: Pol sambol is the king of Sri Lankan sambols, present at every single meal.

method:
1. Slice onions thinly
2. Heat oil, add cinnamon, cardamom
3. Add onions, cook on low heat 20 mins until deep brown
4. Add chili flakes, Maldive fish
5. Add tamarind, sugar, curry leaves
6. Cook 10 more mins until sticky and caramelized
tips: Patience is key - rush the caramelizing and it won't taste right.
cultural_note: Seeni sambol means 'sugar sambol'. This sweet-spicy onion relish is the classic accompaniment to bread and hoppers.

method:
1. Remove stems from dried chilies
2. Roughly chop onion
3. Grind chilies, onion, Maldive fish and salt in mortar and pestle
4. Pound until well combined but still chunky
5. Add lime juice, mix well
6. Serve with kiribath or hoppers
tips: Use a stone mortar and pestle - the texture is better than a blender.
cultural_note: Lunu miris is the fiery chili paste that accompanies kiribath on every auspicious day in Sri Lanka.

method:
1. Pound dried chilies in mortar until flaky
2. Add sliced onions and pound lightly
3. Add Maldive fish, pound briefly
4. Season with lime juice, salt, pepper
5. Mix well
6. Serve as fiery condiment
tips: Pound gently - katta sambol should be chunky, not a paste.
cultural_note: Katta sambol is the hottest of Sri Lankan sambols, a test of spice tolerance!

method:
1. Wash gotukola leaves thoroughly
2. Finely chop leaves, onion, green chili
3. Mix with grated coconut and Maldive fish
4. Add lime juice and salt
5. Toss well
6. Serve fresh as a raw salad
tips: Chop the leaves as finely as possible for best texture.
cultural_note: Gotukola salad is Sri Lanka's most nutritious accompaniment, rich in iron and antioxidants.

method:
1. Blend coconut, green chili, ginger, salt with little water
2. Heat oil, add mustard seeds and urad dhal
3. Add curry leaves when seeds pop
4. Pour tempering over coconut mixture
5. Mix well
6. Serve with dosa, idli, or string hoppers
tips: Keep the chutney slightly coarse for texture.
cultural_note: Coconut chutney is the quintessential Tamil accompaniment served with every South Indian breakfast.

method:
1. Heat oil, add mustard seeds, urad dhal
2. Saute onions, garlic until soft
3. Add chopped tomatoes and dried chilies
4. Cook 10 mins until tomatoes break down
5. Add sugar, salt, mash lightly
6. Cool and serve
tips: Ripe, red tomatoes give the best color and flavor.
cultural_note: Tomato chutney is a tangy accompaniment popular in Tamil households across Northern Sri Lanka.

method:
1. Strip moringa leaves from stems, wash well
2. Finely chop onion and green chili
3. Mix all ingredients together
4. Stir-fry in a dry pan for 5-7 minutes
5. Add coconut and Maldive fish
6. Season with lime juice and salt
tips: Pick only fresh, bright green moringa leaves.
cultural_note: Moringa mallum is a powerhouse of nutrition. Sri Lankan grandmothers call it 'nature's multivitamin'.

method:
1. Wash kangkung, cut into 1-inch pieces
2. Finely chop onion and green chili
3. Heat a dry pan, add kangkung
4. Add onion, chili, turmeric, Maldive fish
5. Stir-fry until wilted, 5-7 minutes
6. Add coconut, lime juice, salt
tips: Do not overcook - kangkung should still be bright green.
cultural_note: Kangkung grows wild near water bodies across Sri Lanka and is one of the most affordable green vegetables.

method:
1. Cut eggplant into thick strips
2. Rub with turmeric and salt, deep fry until golden
3. In same oil, fry onion slices and green chilies
4. Add mustard seeds, chili powder
5. Add vinegar and sugar, cook 2 mins
6. Fold in fried eggplant, coat well
tips: Fry eggplant until well browned for the best texture.
cultural_note: Wambatu moju is an essential component of festive meals, especially at Sinhala New Year.

method:
1. Slice onions into rings
2. Slice green chilies
3. Mix vinegar, sugar, salt, turmeric
4. Add onion rings and chilies
5. Add mustard seeds
6. Marinate at least 1 hour before serving
tips: Make ahead - the flavor improves with time.
cultural_note: Lunudehi is the Sri Lankan version of pickled onions, always served alongside rice and curry at restaurants.

method:
1. Cut fruits into bite-sized sticks
2. Slice onions and green chilies
3. Mix vinegar, sugar, chili powder, mustard paste
4. Combine all fruits, onions, chilies
5. Pour dressing over, toss well
6. Marinate 30 mins before serving
tips: Use firm, unripe fruits for the best crunch.
cultural_note: Achcharu is the beloved Sri Lankan street-side pickle sold by vendors outside schools and temples.

method:
1. Dry roast dried chilies briefly
2. Pound roasted chilies coarsely
3. Add Maldive fish, pound together
4. Mix in finely chopped onion and garlic
5. Heat oil, pour over mixture
6. Add lime juice and salt
tips: Roasting dried chilies enhances their flavor and reduces raw spice heat.
cultural_note: This intensely hot sambol is for true chili lovers and is popular in the Southern Province.

method:
1. Heat oil in a pan
2. Test with small piece of papadam
3. Slide papadam into hot oil
4. Press down with slotted spoon for 3-5 seconds
5. Flip and fry other side 2-3 seconds
6. Drain on paper towels, serve crispy
tips: Oil must be very hot for crispy papadam.
cultural_note: Papadam is the ubiquitous crispy wafer served with every rice and curry meal in Sri Lanka.

method:
1. Cook toor dhal until soft, mash lightly
2. Cook vegetables (drumstick, eggplant, carrot) until tender
3. Add tamarind, sambar powder, turmeric to vegetables
4. Combine with mashed dhal
5. Temper with mustard seeds, curry leaves, garlic
6. Add tempered onions and tomatoes, simmer 10 mins
tips: Use a mix of at least 3 vegetables for best sambar.
cultural_note: Sambar is the essential Tamil side dish, served with rice, dosa, and idli across Northern Sri Lanka.

method:
1. Grate or fine dice cucumber
2. Squeeze out excess water
3. Mix with yogurt
4. Add finely chopped onion, chili, mint
5. Season with salt and cumin
6. Chill before serving
tips: Squeeze excess water from cucumber to keep raita thick.
cultural_note: Raita is a cooling accompaniment that balances spicy curries, common at biriyani meals.

method:
1. Chop dates and soak raisins
2. Heat oil, saute onions, garlic, ginger
3. Add spices, cook 2 mins
4. Add dates, raisins, vinegar, sugar
5. Cook 10 mins until thick and glossy
6. Cool and store in jar
tips: This pickle gets better with age - make it a day ahead.
cultural_note: Malay pickle is a sweet-spicy condiment reflecting the Malay heritage of Sri Lanka's Colombo Malays.

method:
1. Peel and slice kohila stems thinly
2. Boil until tender
3. Add onion, chili, turmeric, Maldive fish
4. Add grated coconut and coconut milk
5. Simmer 10 minutes
6. Season and serve as accompaniment
tips: Peel the rough outer layer of kohila stems before cooking.
cultural_note: Kohila is a unique Sri Lankan aquatic plant with medicinal properties, used in traditional Ayurvedic treatments.

method:
1. Cut vegetables (carrot, beans, pumpkin, snake gourd) into pieces
2. Layer in pot with onion, spices, curry leaves
3. Add thin coconut milk
4. Cook 15-20 mins until tender
5. Add thick coconut milk
6. Simmer 5 mins, season
tips: Use seasonal vegetables and vary combinations.
cultural_note: Elawalu hodda is the everyday mixed vegetable gravy that forms the backbone of the Sri Lankan rice and curry plate.

method:
1. Finely shred gotukola leaves
2. Dice onion and chili very small
3. Mix with scraped coconut
4. Season with salt, pepper, lime
5. Toss all ingredients together
6. Serve fresh
tips: Use only fresh leaves, avoid wilted ones.
cultural_note: This raw pennywort sambol is a staple of Central Province meals.

method:
1. Slice leeks thinly, wash
2. Cook lentils until soft
3. Heat oil, saute onion, leeks
4. Add cooked dhal, Maldive fish, coconut
5. Add chili flakes, turmeric
6. Cook 5 mins, season
tips: Wash leeks thoroughly to remove sand.
cultural_note: Leeks and dhal combination is a nutritious everyday side dish.

method:
1. Wash mint leaves, pick from stems
2. Roughly chop mint, onion, chilies
3. Grind or chop together
4. Add coconut
5. Season with lime juice and salt
6. Serve fresh
tips: Use only fresh mint leaves for best flavor.
cultural_note: Mint sambol is a refreshing accompaniment especially popular with biriyani meals.

method:
1. Slice bitter gourd into thin rounds
2. Rub with salt, turmeric, leave 10 mins
3. Squeeze out bitter juice
4. Dust with rice flour and chili powder
5. Deep fry until crispy
6. Drain and serve
tips: Salt and squeeze to remove max bitterness.
cultural_note: Fried bitter gourd chips are a crunchy, addictive side dish.

method:
1. Fry papadums until crispy
2. Dice tomato and onion small
3. Mix veggies with chili flakes and lime
4. Top each papadum with mixture
5. Serve immediately
6. Eat before they go soft
tips: Top just before serving for crispy results.
cultural_note: Topped papadums are a popular party appetizer and snack.

method:
1. Roughly chop chilies and onion
2. Pound in mortar with salt
3. Add Maldive fish, pound lightly
4. Squeeze lime juice over
5. Mix well
6. Serve in small portions
tips: Use gloves when handling bird's eye chilies.
cultural_note: Kochchi sambol is the most ferociously hot sambol in Sri Lanka, using tiny but potent bird's eye chilies.

method:
1. Grate carrots
2. Finely chop onion and chili
3. Mix all together
4. Add coconut, lime juice, salt
5. Toss well
6. Serve fresh
tips: Use firm, fresh carrots for best crunch.
cultural_note: Carrot sambol adds color and crunch to the plate, and is rich in vitamin A.

method:
1. Slice onions into thin rings
2. Separate into individual rings
3. Mix with chili powder and Maldive fish
4. Add lime juice, salt, pepper
5. Toss well
6. Serve
tips: Let it sit 10 mins for flavors to meld.
cultural_note: A simple but essential sambol served at any quick meal.

method:
1. Wash green chilies
2. Grind chilies, garlic, ginger into paste
3. Add vinegar and salt
4. Mix well
5. Store in clean jar
6. Use as condiment with anything
tips: Sterilize jar properly for longer shelf life.
cultural_note: Green chili paste is a versatile condiment kept in every Sri Lankan kitchen.

method:
1. Peel and dice mango
2. Cook with sugar and vinegar on low heat
3. Add grated ginger, spices
4. Stir regularly for 15-20 mins
5. Cook until thick and glossy
6. Cool, store in sterilized jar
tips: Use ripe but still firm mangoes for best chutney.
cultural_note: Mango chutney reflects colonial influence on Sri Lankan cuisine.

method:
1. Finely chop onion
2. Mix chili powder with sesame oil
3. Add Maldive fish and onion
4. Add lime juice and salt
5. Mix well
6. Serve with string hoppers or pittu
tips: Use dark roasted sesame oil for authentic flavor.
cultural_note: Sesame oil sambol is unique to Tamil cuisine in Northern Sri Lanka.

method:
1. Melt jaggery in warm coconut milk, strain
2. Beat eggs well
3. Mix jaggery-coconut milk with eggs
4. Add cardamom, nutmeg, cloves
5. Pour into mold, top with cashews
6. Steam for 45-60 minutes until set
tips: Do not overbeat eggs - we don't want air bubbles.
cultural_note: Watalappan is Sri Lanka's most beloved dessert, a Malay-origin coconut custard served at every celebration.

method:
1. Mix treacle with coconut milk
2. Add rice flour gradually to make thick batter
3. Rest batter 30 mins
4. Heat oil to medium temperature
5. Pour small amounts, fry on low heat
6. Turn when bottom is golden, cook both sides until dark golden
tips: Temperature control is critical - too hot and outside burns, too cool and they absorb oil.
cultural_note: Kavum is the iconic Sinhala and Tamil New Year sweet, symbolizing prosperity and goodness.

method:
1. Mix rice flour, coconut milk, eggs, sugar, salt, turmeric
2. Make smooth batter (like thick cream consistency)
3. Heat oil, heat kokis mold in oil
4. Dip hot mold into batter (3/4 only!)
5. Immerse in hot oil, shake gently to release
6. Fry until golden and crispy
tips: Never dip the mold fully in batter or it won't release.
cultural_note: Kokis are intricate rosette-shaped cookies of Dutch-Malay origin, an essential New Year treat.

method:
1. Dry roast rice flour until fragrant
2. Heat treacle until it forms a soft ball in water
3. Add roasted flour to treacle, stir vigorously
4. Add ghee, cardamom, chopped cashews
5. Pour into greased tray
6. Cut into diamond shapes before fully cool
tips: The treacle temperature test is crucial - too cool won't set, too hot burns.
cultural_note: Aluwa is a traditional sweet made for the Sinhala New Year, using kithul treacle.

method:
1. Cream butter and sugar until light
2. Add egg yolks one at a time
3. Fold in semolina, chopped cashews, mixed peel
4. Add honey, rose water, spices, lime zest
5. Fold in whipped egg whites gently
6. Bake at 150C for 40-45 mins until golden
tips: Do not overbake - it should be moist and dense.
cultural_note: Love cake is Sri Lanka's most famous Dutch Burgher creation, traditionally made for Christmas and weddings.

method:
1. Mix semolina and grated coconut
2. Melt jaggery, add to mixture
3. Add beaten eggs, butter
4. Fold in chopped cashews, dates, spices
5. Pour into greased baking tin
6. Bake at 170C for 40 minutes
tips: The jaggery gives a deep caramel color and unique flavor.
cultural_note: Bibikkan is a Christmas specialty in Sri Lankan homes, combining local ingredients with European baking.

method:
1. Set thick buffalo curd in clay pot
2. Drizzle kithul treacle generously over curd
3. Serve immediately
4. Mix treacle and curd together when eating
5. Enjoy the sweet-tangy combination
6. May add chopped banana for extra sweetness
tips: Use well-set, thick buffalo curd - not thin yogurt.
cultural_note: Curd and treacle is Sri Lanka's simplest and most beloved dessert, sold at roadside stalls across the country.

method:
1. Peel pumpkin, cut into cubes, remove seeds
2. Soak in lime water overnight to firm up
3. Drain, add to pot with sugar
4. Cook on low heat until translucent
5. Add cardamom, cloves, rose water
6. Cool - the syrup will thicken
tips: Soaking in lime water keeps the pumpkin firm during cooking.
cultural_note: Puhul dosi is a translucent, jewel-like sweetmeat served at weddings and New Year.

method:
1. Extract thick and thin coconut milk
2. Cook thin coconut milk with jaggery until dissolved
3. Add rice flour, stir continuously
4. Cook on low heat for 2-3 hours, stirring constantly
5. Add thick coconut milk, cashews, cardamom
6. Pour into greased tray when mixture pulls away from pot
tips: Continuous stirring for hours is essential - this is a labor of love.
cultural_note: Dodol is a traditional jelly-like sweet of Malay origin, requiring hours of stirring. Making dodol is often a communal family activity.

method:
1. Dry roast rice flour until fragrant
2. Toast grated coconut separately
3. Mix flour and coconut
4. Warm treacle slightly
5. Add treacle to flour mix, combine well
6. Shape into small balls while still warm
tips: Shape quickly while warm - the mixture hardens as it cools.
cultural_note: Aggala are simple rice and treacle balls traditionally made by grandmothers for children as a healthy snack.

method:
1. Extract coconut milk in stages
2. Dissolve jaggery in thin coconut milk
3. Add rice flour, stir continuously
4. Cook 3+ hours on very low heat
5. Add thick coconut milk gradually
6. Pour into tray when very thick and dark
tips: The darker it gets, the better the flavor.
cultural_note: Kalu dodol is the prestigious dark version of dodol, a must-have at Sinhala New Year.

method:
1. Mix coconut, condensed milk, sugar, vanilla
2. Cook on low heat stirring constantly until thick
3. Divide into two portions
4. Add pink coloring to one half
5. Press white layer into tray, then pink on top
6. Cool and cut into squares
tips: Press layers firmly for clean-cut squares.
cultural_note: Coconut ice is a beloved Sri Lankan sweet often sold at school fairs and charity events.

method:
1. Combine sugar, milk, butter in heavy pot
2. Cook on medium heat, stirring constantly
3. When mixture reaches soft ball stage (115C)
4. Remove from heat, add vanilla and cashews
5. Beat vigorously until thick and creamy
6. Pour into greased tray, cut while warm
tips: Use a candy thermometer for perfect texture.
cultural_note: Milk toffee is Sri Lanka's most popular homemade sweet, made by every household for celebrations.

method:
1. Soak sago in water 30 mins, drain
2. Cook sago in thin coconut milk until translucent
3. Add sugar, cardamom
4. Add thick coconut milk
5. Pour into molds, top with cashews and raisins
6. Chill until set
tips: Soak sago well to reduce cooking time.
cultural_note: Sago pudding is a cooling dessert popular across Sri Lanka, especially during hot weather.

method:
1. Cut all fruits into bite-sized pieces
2. Scoop passion fruit pulp
3. Combine all fruits in bowl
4. Sprinkle sugar and lime juice
5. Toss gently
6. Serve chilled, optionally with ice cream
tips: Use ripe, seasonal tropical fruits for best flavor.
cultural_note: Sri Lanka's tropical fruit salad showcases the island's incredible diversity of fresh fruits.

method:
1. Mix flour, coconut, grated jaggery, cardamom
2. Add water to make thick paste
3. Place spoonfuls on kenda leaves
4. Wrap into parcels
5. Steam for 15-20 minutes
6. Serve in the leaf wrapping
tips: Use fresh kenda leaves for the aromatic wrapping.
cultural_note: Helapa is an ancient sweet wrapped in wild ginger leaves, giving it a unique aroma.

method:
1. Soak falooda seeds in water until expanded
2. Cook falooda noodles, cool
3. Cut jelly into cubes
4. Layer in glass: noodles, seeds, jelly
5. Pour sweetened rose milk over
6. Top with ice cream
tips: Layer in a tall glass for visual appeal.
cultural_note: Falooda is a beloved Sri Lankan dessert drink of Indian-Malay origin, sold at every sweet shop.

method:
1. Mix both flours
2. Add treacle and coconut milk to form thick batter
3. Add cardamom
4. Rest for 30 minutes
5. Drop spoonfuls into medium-hot oil
6. Fry slowly until dark golden brown
tips: Low temperature frying is essential for cooked centers.
cultural_note: Mung kavum are the green gram variety of the Traditional New Year kavum.

method:
1. Cream butter and sugar until fluffy
2. Add egg yolks one at a time
3. Fold in flour and spices
4. Add brandy and vanilla
5. Whip egg whites, fold in gently
6. Bake in layers under a grill, one layer at a time
tips: Each layer must be individually grilled for the striped effect.
cultural_note: Bolo fiado is a Portuguese-origin layer cake unique to Sri Lankan Burgher Christmas celebrations.

method:
1. Dissolve yeast in warm milk with sugar
2. Mix flour, semolina, spices
3. Add beaten eggs, melted butter
4. Knead into soft dough, add mixed peel
5. Prove 2 hours until doubled
6. Shape, prove again, bake at 180C for 40 mins
tips: Double proving is essential for light, fluffy bread.
cultural_note: Breudher is the Dutch-Burgher Christmas bread, a beloved holiday tradition unique to Sri Lanka.

method:
1. Mix flour, coconut, scraped jaggery, salt
2. Add water sparingly to form stiff dough
3. Shape into thick rounds
4. Cook on griddle both sides until done
5. The jaggery melts creating sweet spots
6. Serve warm
tips: Do not add too much water - dough should be very stiff.
cultural_note: A sweet variation of the classic pol roti, enjoyed as a snack or dessert.

method:
1. Dry roast semolina in butter until golden
2. Heat milk, add sugar
3. Add roasted semolina slowly, stirring constantly
4. Add cardamom, cashews, raisins
5. Cook until thick, pour into mold
6. Chill and serve
tips: Roasting semolina first gives a nutty flavor.
cultural_note: Semolina pudding is a quick, easy dessert loved by children across Sri Lanka.

method:
1. Chop dates, soak in hot water 15 mins
2. Cream butter and sugar
3. Add eggs one at a time
4. Fold in flour and baking powder
5. Add dates, chopped cashews, vanilla, cinnamon
6. Bake at 170C for 40-45 minutes
tips: Use soft Medjool-style dates for moist cake.
cultural_note: Date and nut cake is popular during the Christmas season in Sri Lankan homes.

method:
1. Soak urad dhal, grind to paste
2. Mix with rice flour and water to make thick batter
3. Fill batter into squeeze bottle or coconut shell with holes
4. Pipe spirals into medium-hot oil
5. Fry until golden
6. Dip in warm treacle, drain
tips: Pipe into oil in spiral shapes - practice makes perfect.
cultural_note: Pani walalu are golden spiral-shaped sweets dipped in kithul treacle, a quintessential New Year treat.

method:
1. Caramelize half the sugar with water until dark amber
2. Pour into mold, swirl to coat bottom
3. Beat eggs with remaining sugar
4. Heat milk (don't boil), add vanilla
5. Mix egg mixture into milk, strain into mold
6. Steam or bake in water bath 50-60 mins until set
tips: Strain the custard for ultra-smooth texture.
cultural_note: Caramel pudding is the most popular dessert at Sri Lankan restaurants and parties.

method:
1. Saute garlic, add mince, cook until done
2. Add vegetables, soy sauce, cook 5 mins
3. Cool the filling completely
4. Place filling on wrapper, roll tightly
5. Seal with egg wash, coat in breadcrumbs
6. Deep fry until golden brown
tips: Cool filling completely before wrapping to prevent soggy rolls.
cultural_note: Chinese rolls are Sri Lanka's most popular short eat, found in every bakery across the island.

method:
1. Mash potatoes, flake fish
2. Saute onions, chili, curry leaves
3. Mix fish, potato, sauteed mixture, chili powder, lime
4. Shape into patties
5. Dip in beaten egg, coat with breadcrumbs
6. Deep fry until golden
tips: Shape cutlets uniformly for even cooking.
cultural_note: Fish cutlets are the quintessential Sri Lankan party snack, served at every celebration.

method:
1. Cook mutton mince with spices until dry
2. Boil and mash potatoes, mix with mince
3. Make thin pancakes from flour batter
4. Place filling on pancake, roll into cylinder
5. Coat in egg and breadcrumbs
6. Deep fry until golden crispy
tips: The filling must be completely dry to prevent bursting.
cultural_note: Mutton rolls are the premium version of the Sri Lankan roll, a bakery highlight.

method:
1. Soak urad dhal 4 hours, grind to thick paste
2. Mix in chopped onion, chili, curry leaves
3. Shape into flat rounds
4. Place a whole prawn on top of each
5. Deep fry until golden
6. Drain and serve hot
tips: Press the prawn firmly into the batter so it doesn't fall off.
cultural_note: Isso wade are the iconic prawn-topped fritters from Galle Face Green, Colombo's famous seaside promenade.

method:
1. Cook mince with spices, onion, diced potato
2. Cool filling completely
3. Roll out puff pastry, cut into circles
4. Place filling, fold into half-moons
5. Seal edges with fork, brush with egg wash
6. Bake at 200C for 20-25 mins until puffed and golden
tips: Chill pastry between steps for flakier results.
cultural_note: Patties are a colonial-era introduction that became a beloved Sri Lankan bakery staple.

method:
1. Soak dhal 4 hours, drain
2. Grind to thick, fluffy paste without water
3. Add chopped onion, chili, ginger, curry leaves, pepper
4. Shape into donut shape with hole in center
5. Deep fry in medium-hot oil
6. Fry until deep golden brown on both sides
tips: The paste must be thick - add no water while grinding.
cultural_note: Ulundu vadai is a classic Jaffna Tamil snack, traditionally offered at Hindu temples.

method:
1. Cook mince filling with spices
2. Peel hard-boiled eggs
3. Wrap each egg with mince filling
4. Wrap in pancake wrapper
5. Coat in beaten egg and breadcrumbs
6. Deep fry until golden
tips: Let the coated eggs chill 15 mins before frying for better adhesion.
cultural_note: Egg rolls are a popular party snack that combines the beloved Sri Lankan roll with a surprise egg center.

method:
1. Mix flour, coconut, salt
2. Add finely chopped vegetables
3. Add water to make stiff dough
4. Shape into thick rounds
5. Cook on hot griddle without oil
6. Cook both sides till done
tips: Chop vegetables very fine so they cook through.
cultural_note: Vegetable roti is a nutritious quick snack perfect for tea time.

method:
1. Make pastry dough with flour, oil, water
2. Boil and mash potatoes roughly
3. Saute onions, cumin, add potato, peas, spices
4. Roll dough into circles, cut in half
5. Form cone shape, fill with potato mixture, seal
6. Deep fry until golden and crispy
tips: Seal edges with water paste to prevent opening during frying.
cultural_note: Samosas in Sri Lanka are influenced by both Indian and Malay culinary traditions.

method:
1. Cut all fruits into stick shapes
2. Thread onto wooden skewers
3. Mix chili powder, salt, sugar, vinegar into paste
4. Brush or dip fruit sticks into spice paste
5. Arrange on plate
6. Serve immediately
tips: Use firm, unripe fruits for best crunch.
cultural_note: Achcharu sticks are the favorite after-school snack sold by vendors outside every school in Sri Lanka.

method:
1. Dissolve yeast in warm milk with sugar
2. Mix flour, salt, add yeast mixture and egg
3. Knead in butter until smooth dough
4. Prove 1 hour until doubled
5. Shape into crocodile form (elongated with pointed ends)
6. Prove again 20 mins, bake at 180C for 20-25 mins
tips: The classic shape has a bulge in the middle tapering at both ends.
cultural_note: Kimbula banis (crocodile buns) are a uniquely Sri Lankan bread shape, a beloved bakery item since colonial times.

method:
1. Dissolve yeast in warm water with sugar
2. Mix flour and salt
3. Add yeast mixture and oil, knead 10 mins
4. Prove 1 hour until doubled
5. Shape into loaf, place in greased tin
6. Prove 30 mins, bake at 200C for 25-30 mins
tips: Knead thoroughly for proper gluten development.
cultural_note: Roast paan is Sri Lanka's signature bread, baked in wood-fired ovens in local bakeries every morning.

method:
1. Peel manioc, slice into thin chips
2. Soak in salted turmeric water 10 mins
3. Drain and pat dry thoroughly
4. Deep fry in batches until golden and crispy
5. Drain on paper
6. Season with chili powder and salt while hot
tips: Slice uniformly thin for even crisping. Dry well before frying.
cultural_note: Manioc chips are a popular tea-time snack and a crunchy accompaniment to drinks.

method:
1. Peel raw bananas
2. Slice thinly using a mandoline
3. Soak in salted turmeric water
4. Drain and dry completely
5. Deep fry until crispy and light golden
6. Drain, cool, store in airtight container
tips: Use a mandoline slicer for uniformly thin chips.
cultural_note: Banana chips are Sri Lanka's favorite crispy snack, perfect with evening tea.

method:
1. Soak chickpeas overnight, boil until tender
2. Drain well
3. Finely chop onion and green chili
4. Mix chickpeas with onion, chili, coconut
5. Season with chili powder, lime juice, salt
6. Toss well and serve
tips: Boil chickpeas with a pinch of baking soda for softer texture.
cultural_note: Kadala salad is the popular street-food snack sold at cricket matches and bus stands across Sri Lanka.

method:
1. Prepare spiced meat or fish filling, cool
2. Divide bread dough into 8 portions
3. Flatten each, place filling in center with egg half
4. Seal edges, shape into bun
5. Prove 20 mins
6. Bake at 190C for 18-20 mins until golden
tips: Seal edges tightly to prevent filling from leaking.
cultural_note: Mas paan is the beloved Sri Lankan stuffed bun, an afternoon snack institution.

method:
1. Mix treacle with coconut milk
2. Add rice flour and baking soda to form thick batter
3. Rest 20 minutes
4. Heat oil to medium temperature
5. Pour batter slowly, the hair-like strands form as it flows
6. Fry until dark golden
tips: The batter must flow in thin strands to create the signature 'hair' appearance.
cultural_note: Konda kavum get their name from the hair-like strands that form during frying, a traditional New Year sweet.

method:
1. Mix flour, coconut, onion, Maldive fish, chili, salt
2. Add water to make thick dough
3. Place portions on banana leaf squares
4. Wrap into parcels
5. Steam 15-20 minutes
6. Serve in leaf wrapping
tips: Soften banana leaves over flame before wrapping.
cultural_note: Lavariya petti are traditional steamed rice parcels from Southern Sri Lanka.

method:
1. Soak chana dhal 3 hours, grind coarsely
2. Mix in chopped onion, chili, fennel, curry leaves
3. Shape into flat rounds
4. Press a small prawn on top of each
5. Deep fry until crispy golden
6. Drain and serve hot with chutney
tips: Grind dhal coarsely for crunchy texture.
cultural_note: Jaffna-style isso vadai uses chana dhal instead of urad dhal, giving a crunchier texture.

method:
1. Soak chana dhal 3 hours
2. Grind very coarsely (chunky)
3. Mix with chopped onion, chili, curry leaves, pepper
4. Shape into flat rounds
5. Deep fry until crispy golden
6. Drain and serve
tips: Keep the grind very coarse for maximum crunch.
cultural_note: Dhal wade is a crunchy lentil fritter enjoyed across Sri Lanka as a snack.

method:
1. Stir-fry vegetables with garlic and soy sauce
2. Drain excess liquid, cool completely
3. Place filling on wrapper corner
4. Roll tightly, folding in sides
5. Seal with flour paste
6. Deep fry until crispy golden
tips: Drain vegetables well - wet filling makes soggy rolls.
cultural_note: Spring rolls are a beloved snack at Sri Lankan Chinese restaurants.

method:
1. Boil and mash potatoes
2. Finely dice and cook carrots, peas, beans
3. Saute onion, chili, curry leaves
4. Mix everything with mashed potato
5. Shape, dip in egg, coat breadcrumbs
6. Deep fry until golden
tips: Ensure vegetables are cooked but firm, not mushy.
cultural_note: Vegetable cutlets are the vegetarian option at every Sri Lankan bakery and party.

method:
1. Heat oil to very hot (180C)
2. Drop a few crackers at a time
3. They will puff up instantly in 3-5 seconds
4. Turn once if needed
5. Remove immediately when puffed
6. Drain on paper and serve
tips: Oil must be very hot - crackers should puff instantly.
cultural_note: Prawn crackers are a popular appetizer served at Sri Lankan Chinese restaurants.

method:
1. Make pastry: rub butter into flour, add water for dough
2. Mix coconut, jaggery, cardamom for filling
3. Roll pastry thin, cut into circles
4. Place filling, fold into half-moons
5. Seal edges with fork
6. Deep fry until golden
tips: Seal edges well to keep filling inside.
cultural_note: Pol petti are traditional coconut-filled pastries, a beloved sweet snack.

method:
1. Peel tapioca, slice paper-thin
2. Wash and soak in turmeric water
3. Drain and dry completely
4. Deep fry in batches until crispy
5. Season with salt and chili powder
6. Cool and store airtight
tips: Slice extremely thin for maximum crispness.
cultural_note: Tapioca chips are a crunchy snack common in Southern and Sabaragamuwa provinces.

method:
1. Warm treacle slightly, mix with coconut milk
2. Add rice flour gradually to make thick batter
3. Rest batter 1 hour
4. Heat oil to medium (not too hot)
5. Pour small amounts using coconut shell ladle
6. Fry very slowly, turning, until deep golden brown
tips: Low and slow frying is the secret - rush it and outside burns.
cultural_note: Kavum is THE iconic Sinhala and Tamil New Year sweet, made on April 13th every year. The first kavum is ceremonially fried at the auspicious time.

method:
1. Mix all ingredients to smooth batter like thick cream
2. Heat oil, heat kokis mold in oil
3. Dip hot mold in batter to 3/4 depth only
4. Immerse in oil, hold until kokis releases
5. Fry until golden and crispy
6. Drain and cool completely
tips: If mold goes fully in batter, the kokis won't release from the mold.
cultural_note: Kokis are the crispy, flower-shaped cookies essential to the New Year table. Different families have different ancestral mold designs.

method:
1. Wash rice and cook with water until half done
2. Add thick coconut milk generously
3. Cook on low heat, stirring gently
4. Press into tray, smooth with wet spoon
5. Cut into diamond shapes
6. Serve at the auspicious New Year time with lunu miris
tips: Make extra milk rice - it is the first food of the New Year.
cultural_note: Kiribath is ceremonially cooked at the exact auspicious time on Sinhala New Year morning, symbolizing prosperity for the coming year.

method:
1. Grate jaggery, dissolve in minimal water
2. Strain and cook until thick syrup
3. Mix with rice flour to form stiff dough
4. Rest overnight for better flavor
5. Shape into flat rounds, press sesame seeds on top
6. Deep fry on low heat until dark golden
tips: Overnight resting makes the perfect texture.
cultural_note: Athirasa is an ancient festival sweet, predating even kavum in Sri Lankan culinary history.

method:
1. Mix flour, sugar, coconut milk, cardamom
2. Divide dough into portions, color each differently
3. Shape into small balls and crescents
4. Deep fry until golden
5. Drain well
6. Arrange colorfully on plate like lantern shapes
tips: Use natural food coloring for healthy treats.
cultural_note: These colorful sweets are made during Vesak festival to share with communities celebrating at temples.

method:
1. Marinate lamb overnight in yogurt and spices
2. Cook lamb until tender in its marinade
3. Parboil rice with whole spices
4. Deep fry onions until dark brown
5. Layer: lamb, rice, fried onions, herbs, saffron milk
6. Seal pot, dum cook 30 minutes on lowest heat
tips: Overnight marination is non-negotiable for Eid biriyani.
cultural_note: Eid biriyani is the centerpiece of Sri Lankan Muslim celebrations, prepared in massive quantities to feed families and neighbors.

method:
1. Dry roast moong dhal until golden
2. Cook rice and dhal together until soft
3. Melt jaggery in water, strain
4. Add jaggery syrup to cooked rice
5. Add coconut milk, cardamom, cook 10 mins
6. Temper cashews and raisins in ghee, garnish
tips: Use freshly harvested rice for authentic Thai Pongal.
cultural_note: Thai Pongal celebrates the Tamil harvest festival in January. The rice is ceremonially boiled and allowed to overflow the pot, symbolizing abundance.

method:
1. Soak dried fruits in brandy for 2 weeks
2. Cream butter and sugar until very light
3. Add eggs one at a time, then treacle
4. Fold in flour, semolina, spices
5. Add soaked fruits, nuts, preserves
